combat_history.event: No combat history - first ship not yet commissioned. Program currently in construction phase with HMAS Hunter in early build stages.
combat_history.significance: Platform remains unproven in operational conditions, with capabilities based on design specifications and Type 26 baseline performance

modernization.description: Planned spiral development approach to incorporate new technologies and address capability gaps identified during construction and early operations. May include enhanced electronic warfare systems, directed energy weapons, and improved cyber resilience.
modernization.impact: Will maintain technological relevance throughout the production run and early service life

modernization.description: Integration of select AEGIS combat system elements with SAAB 9LV, including improved cooperative engagement capability and ballistic missile defense potential.
modernization.impact: Enhanced interoperability with U.S. Navy and improved area air defense capabilities

vulnerabilities.description: Extensive schedule delays, cost overruns exceeding 20%, and complex systems integration challenges. First ship delivery pushed from 2027 to 2031.
vulnerabilities.context: Australia needs these capabilities urgently given regional threat development, but delays leave capability gaps
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Government considering interim measures including additional Hobart-class destroyers or accelerated submarine programs

vulnerabilities.description: 7,000+ tonne displacement and complex systems may be excessive for Australia's actual operational needs, creating sustainment burden.
vulnerabilities.context: Designed for high-intensity warfare but may spend most time on constabulary duties in Australia's EEZ
vulnerabilities.mitigation: None identified - committed to current design

vulnerabilities.description: Only 32 VLS cells compared to 48+ on peer platforms, limiting sustained engagement capability in high-threat environments.
vulnerabilities.context: In South China Sea scenarios, limited missile loadout could be problematic for extended operations
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Reliance on cooperative engagement and task group integration to provide additional firepower

vulnerabilities.description: New shipyard, inexperienced workforce, and complex technology transfer creating quality and schedule risks.
vulnerabilities.context: Australia rebuilding naval shipbuilding capability from near-zero baseline
vulnerabilities.mitigation: Extensive BAE Systems oversight and gradual workforce development programs

operational_patterns.typical_deployment: Independent patrol operations in Australia's EEZ, task group operations with allies in Indo-Pacific, ASW screening for high-value units
operational_patterns.typical_task_group: Operates with Hobart-class destroyers, Collins-class submarines, replenishment ships, or integrated with U.S./UK task groups
operational_patterns.readiness_notes: Sustainment model still being developed; concerns about maintenance capability and parts availability for complex hybrid systems

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Hunter-class features different radar (AN/SPY-6 vs Type 997), combat system (9LV vs CMS-1), and weapons fit (NSM vs Sea Ceptor). Australian version is larger and more complex.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: How much customization is too much - comparing baseline Type 26 to heavily modified Hunter variant

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Both are Type 26 derivatives but Canadian version features different mission bay utilization, potentially larger VLS fit, and different radar solutions. Similar timeline challenges.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Tale of two Type 26 programs - how different allies adapted the same baseline design

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Type 055 is much larger (13,000 tonnes), carries 112 VLS cells, and has dual-band radar. Represents the high-end threat Hunter-class may face in South China Sea scenarios.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: David vs Goliath - how Hunter-class frigates would engage Type 055 destroyers in contested scenarios

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Constellation-class is smaller (7,300 tonnes), based on FREMM design, with 32 VLS cells but different sensor suite. Both programs face similar schedule and cost challenges.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: Allied frigate programs - comparing U.S. and Australian approaches to next-generation surface combatants

peer_comparisons.key_differences: Mogami is lighter (5,500 tonnes), more focused on ASW with advanced sonar, but less area air defense capability. Different operational philosophy for different threat environment.
peer_comparisons.video_angle: ASW specialist comparison - different approaches to submarine hunting in the Pacific
